coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodots synthesized from used tobacco molasses as promising selective probes for Fe (III) ion sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Sawalha, Shadi
Assali, Mohyeddin
Yaseen, Amal
Ataya, Aya
Refai, Leena
Hamed, Rinad
Misia, Giuseppe
Collavini, Silvia
Silvestri, Alessandro
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) as an emerging and new class of nanomaterials have been widely used as sensing probes for metal cations based on fluorescence quenching. Several precursors have been utilized as starting materials for the synthesis of CNDs, either natural, artificial, or waste materials. Herein, we present a new precursor and cost-effective method to synthesize CNDs aligning with circular economy and green chemistry principles. Two classes of CNDs were obtained, starting from used (smoked) tobacco molasses, through two processes. First, extraction was directly carried out on molasses residue to isolate one class of CNDs, i.e. D-CNDs; then the residual solid was treated by chemical oxidation, yielding a different population of CNDs, named O-CNDs. Synthesized CNDs exhibited different properties in terms of size, functional groups, and optical properties. These features lead to different sensing abilities toward Fe3+ cation. O-CNDs showed better results in terms of sensitivity (0.0045 μM−1), limit of detection (LOD = 3.9 μM), and limit of quantification (LOQ = 13 μM) compared to D-CNDs (Sensitivity = 0.0029 μM−1, LOD = 7.2 μM, and LOQ = 24 μM). The quenching mechanism attained in both CNDs is due to a combined static-dynamic effect in which the static is the dominant component. Therefore, the synthesized O-CNDs could serve as promising and low-cost nanomaterials for selective sensing of Fe3+ cation in contaminated water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent sensor
Green chemistry
Fe3+ cation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing activity of the synthesized CNDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) AFM image, (b) Distribution histogram, (c) Height profile of D-CNDs, (d) AFM image, (e) Distribution histogram, and (f) Height profile of O-CNDs.
(a) FTIR spectra of synthesized D-CNDs and O-CNDs, (b) XPS survey of D- CNDs, and (c) XPS survey of O-CNDs.
High-resolution XPS spectra of (a) C 1s of D-CNDs, (b) O 1s of D-CNDs, (c) C 1s of O-CNDs, and (d) O 1s of O-CNDs.
(a) The absorption spectra of prepared D-CNDs, (b) Tauc plot of D-CNDs, (c) The absorption spectra of prepared O-CNDs, (d) Tauc plot of O-CNDs.

(a) D-CNDs fluorescence quenching upon cation ions spiking (b) D-CNDs selectivity of different cations (c) O-CNDs fluorescence quenching upon cation ions spiking (d) O-CNDs selectivity of different cations.

(a) The fluorescence quenching of O-CNDs at different [Fe3+] (b)) The fluorescence quenching of D-CNDs at different [Fe3+] (c) Quenching efficiency curves for D-CNDs and O-CNDs (d) Fitted calibration curves.
Quenching Mechanism of CNDs with Fe+3 (stern Volmer plot) a) O- CNDs, b) D-CNDs.
Sensing parameters of D-CNDs and O-CNDs for Fe3+ ion.
Fe+3 sensing with CNDs in real water samples.
Comparison of synthesized D-CNDs and O-CNDs as Fe+3 sensors with other CNDs synthesized from wastes.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots synthesized from used tobacco molasses as promising selective probes for Fe (III) ion sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Molecular Sciences and Nanosystems, Ca' Foscari University of Venice, Venezia, 30170 Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) as an emerging and new class of nanomaterials have been widely used as sensing probes for metal cations based on fluorescence quenching. Several precursors have been utilized as starting materials for the synthesis of CNDs, either natural, artificial, or waste materials. Herein, we present a new precursor and cost-effective method to synthesize CNDs aligning with circular economy and green chemistry principles. Two classes of CNDs were obtained, starting from used (smoked) tobacco molasses, through two processes. First, extraction was directly carried out on molasses residue to isolate one class of CNDs, i.e. D-CNDs; then the residual solid was treated by chemical oxidation, yielding a different population of CNDs, named O-CNDs. Synthesized CNDs exhibited different properties in terms of size, functional groups, and optical properties. These features lead to different sensing abilities toward Fe3+ cation. O-CNDs showed better results in terms of sensitivity (0.0045 μM−1), limit of detection (LOD = 3.9 μM), and limit of quantification (LOQ = 13 μM) compared to D-CNDs (Sensitivity = 0.0029 μM−1, LOD = 7.2 μM, and LOQ = 24 μM). The quenching mechanism attained in both CNDs is due to a combined static-dynamic effect in which the static is the dominant component. Therefore, the synthesized O-CNDs could serve as promising and low-cost nanomaterials for selective sensing of Fe3+ cation in contaminated water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The global environmental challenge posed by diversely generated waste is monitored with increasing apprehension. The accumulation of waste can lead to resource depletion, pollution, and biodiversity degradation [1]. Within this context, the circular economy is pivotal in mitigating these detrimental impacts through waste management strategies based on tangible green transaction pathways by embracing enduring design, maintenance, repair, reuse, remanufacturing, and recycling [2]. In 2020, the European Commission extolled the circular economy virtues as a solution for sustainable environmental growth for recovering waste and giving it a second life as a new product in different applications [3]. These green regenerative systems also minimize raw resource usage, waste, emissions, and energy loss. In this way, it will be a feasible eco-friendly life cycle of waste as a new product, which will be extended and thus foster resilience and sustainability for the future [4].

Both from the economic and environmental point of view, wastes generated from agricultural residues, biomass, or organic waste have been utilized in this manner for their low cost, ease of recycling process, and minimization of their bioaccumulation. Green chemistry research has recently been dedicated to converting these biowastes of carbon-based materials into highly demanded carbonaceous nanomaterials like carbon nanodots (CNDs) or carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [5]. These CNDs expose unique size, surface state, and optical properties, leading to diverse implementations in enormous applications, such as bio-imaging, electrochemical, drug delivery, catalysis, sensing, and other applications [5–7].

CNDs are spherical particles with diminutive dimensions (typically <10 nm) and exhibit intense tunable fluorescence emission within the visible and near-infrared spectrum [8,9]. CNDs are particularly valuable in their non-toxic nature and biocompatibility, allowing for the attachment of various functional groups such as amine, carbonyl, epoxy, carboxyl, ether, and hydroxyl to their surface state [10]. These surface functional groups can be modified to tailor their desired properties and applications through conventional synthesis like pyrolysis, hydrothermal, microwave, etc. [11].

The excellent optical properties of CNDs such as good fluorescence and the existence of different surface functional groups, allow for utilizing these CNDs in sensing applications through the affinity with metallic ions or impurity molecules, which leads to quenching of fluorescence. The capability of sensing is measured by the selectivity, stability, sensitivity, and limit of detection (LOD) of the developed sensor [12,13]. Heavy metal ions are considered highly toxic pollutants in water resources, and their detection is still a pressing topic, as they harm human health and ecosystem preservation [14]. Agricultural operations and industrial practices contribute significantly to the increase in inorganic heavy metals element levels such as Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, and Pb2+ ions, which exceed the recommended threshold established by the World Health Organization (WHO) [15,16]. Iron ions (Fe2+, Fe3+) are water reservoirs' most abundant heavy metals; large quantities of these ions will induce harmful human diseases like neurological complications, such as Parkinson's and Alzheimer's, and immunological defects [17]. For this reason, several synthesized green CNDs materials have recently been utilized as selective sensor probes for monitoring a wide range of possibilities in situ and multi-heavy metal detection [18].

The synthesis and extraction of green CNDs as fluorescent sensors derived from natural precursors is a highly researched subject aligned with the circular economy purposes, encompassing a variety of sources, including food and plant waste such as willow bark, watermelon bark, plant leaves, eggshell membrane, rice husk, coffee grounds, and willow bark or artificial biowaste as candle soot, humic acid, citric acid, glucose, graphite, olive waste, and cigarette filters, etc. [19–25]. This intersection of CNDs particle synthesis and waste management contributes to sustainable solutions that enhance scientific innovation and environmental perspectives.

Tobacco molasses waste is one of these viable green precursors that may be used to produce green CNDs. The tobacco molasses used in smoking the narghile in the Middle East is a blend of tobacco leaves, sugar, flavor, trace dye, and glycerin [26]. The waste generated from used tobacco molasses cannot undergo regeneration and encompasses harmful and toxic chemicals that can accumulate in soils and aquatic systems, contaminating plants, food, animals, and water reservoirs. Therefore, recycling this waste is necessary for a more convenient product to minimize its environmental impact. One innovative and promising approach is converting this waste into CNDs. This facile process follows most fundamental circular economy concerns and a sustainable economic environmental solution [18,27–29].

In this study, we explore tobacco molasses as a viable precursor for the green synthesis method of two CNDs particles with different properties and applicability for sensing heavy metals. The green synthesis technique of both CNDs relies on a straightforward route to recycle waste without the aid or consumption of toxic chemicals, where it simply employs water as a solvent and operates at low temperatures which aligns with green chemistry principles. To the best of our knowledge, this synthetic technique is considered the first stride that tobacco molasses is used through green transaction procedure for producing two types of fluorescent CNDs probes, and represents a significant instance towards achieving circular economy and sustainable environmental transaction. The performance of both green CNDs probes for Fe3+ detection has been evaluated through selected iron–detecting parameters as fluorescence quenching responses, selectivity, sensitivity, sensing range, limit of detection (LOD), and limit of quantification (LOQ). This highly green, harmonious cycle of waste recycling paves the way for a more resilient, low emission, less raw materials usage, and environmentally conscious future.

Used (smoked) tobacco molasses was collected from the local market and used as a precursor for the synthesis of CNDs. The necessary chemicals and reagents were all purchased from Sigma Aldrich and used directly without purification. Distilled water was used as a media in preparing CNDs and diluting ion salts for the sensing applications.

UV–Vis spectra from 200 to 800 nm were recorded by UV–Vis spectrophotometry with a Beckman Coulter DU 800 model. In the emission range of 320–650 nm, steady-state fluorescence measurements were acquired at different excitation wavelengths, from 300 to 440 nm with 20 nm increment, by PerkinElmer LS50B Fluorometer. The size and morphology of prepared CNDs were studied and analyzed using a Nanosurf Core AFM microscope and Gwyddion software. AFM samples were prepared by drop-casting of diluted solutions of CNDs on mica substrates and then dried under vacuum at 110 °C. Fourier Transform Infrared spectra of synthetic CNDs in powder were captured in the range of 4000–600 cm−1 with 8 cm−1 resolution and 32 scans using a Thermo Scientific Nicolet IS5 FTIR attached with ATR sampling equipment. ζ-potential measurements were performed using phase analysis light scattering (PALS) for all samples by Nano Brook Omni instrument. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) acquisitions were conducted by PHI 5000 VersaProbe III photoelectron spectrometer. For XPS samples, CNDs solutions were drop casted on a gold substrate and dried under vacuum for 24 h.

In this work, CNDs have been synthesized by two methods; Extraction, and chemical oxidation. Extraction is a method used to extract CNDs from natural resources, biomass, or precursors containing originally CNDs such as instant coffee or beet molasses which might be formed by previous occured pyrolysis steps [30]. Several process conditions in extraction could affect the properties of synthesized CNDs, these conditions are, extraction solvent, the ratio of the precursor to solvent, and soaking time. Firstly, collected used (smoked) tobacco molasses (TM) was soaked in distilled water as extraction solvent with different ratios 1:8, 1:16, 1:24, and 1:32, and for different soaking times (6, 12, 18, and 24 h) getting best properties for a ratio of 1:24 (TM:H2O) and 24 h soaking time at room temperature. The resulting solution was filtered through a microfilter paper to collect the liquid part and then centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min to remove large residual particles. The extracted solution (the supernatant) was kept in the dark and named as D-CNDs. Residual solid tobacco molasses dried for 1 h at 100 °C and then cooled to room temperature. The tobacco powder was hand-milled using mortar and pestle. 0.1 g Tobacco molasses powder was mixed with 10 ml distilled water and then sonicated for 5 min. Next, 150 μl of H2O2 was added to the previous solution and sonicated again for 15 min. After that, the solution was refluxed for 90 min. After the reaction was finished, the resulting solution was centrifuged for 10 min at 4000 rpm using universal 320 centrifuges. A 0.2 μm microfilter was used to filter the final supernatant. The resulting CNDs solution was named O-CNDs. Herein, the O-CNDs have been synthesized by the chemical oxidation method which is mainly suitable for carbon-based precursors with the aid of oxidant agents such as strong acids; HSO4, HNO3, and H2O2. The aforementioned conditions (amount of hydrogen peroxide, amount of precursor, and refluxing time) have been selected according to previously conducted studies [8,22,31].

The solutions were freeze-dried to calculate the product yield (D-CNDs, O-CNDs), and the obtained powder was kept for further applications and characterization. The process of producing both CNDs is summarized in Fig. 1 .

The extraction and chemical oxidation methods affect the final properties of synthesized CNDs based on the selected process variables. In the extraction method which is considered a direct approach for synthesis of CNDs, the CNDs properties are affected mainly by the properties of the precursor and the structure of carbon-based material formed previously by other steps such as pyrolysis, burning, or roasting. In some cases, the CNDs properties might be affected by the used extraction solvent such as fluorescence due to the interaction nature between the solvent and CNDs’ particles [32]. In chemical oxidation, the use of oxidant agents enhances the breakdown of large carbon sources into tiny particles and increases the amount of oxygen groups which affect optical properties such as energy band gap and fluorescence [33] as will be discussed later.

The following Equation (Eq. (1)) was used to determine the fluorescence quantum yield (%QY) of CNDs [34].
(1)
Q Y =
Q R
I
I R
A R
A
n 2
n R 2

QY is the quantum yield, I is the integrated emission intensity at 360 nm excitation wavelength, n is the refractive index, and A is the absorption of the samples. R refers to the reference fluorophore of known quantum yield, Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QR = 55 % at 360 nm). For water, n is equal to 1.33. To minimize re-absorption effects, the absorbance in the 10 mm cuvette was kept under 0.1 at the excitation wavelength (360 nm).

500 μM metal ion solutions (Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, and Pb2+) were prepared in deionized water using their respective salts (FeCl3, FeCl2, MnCl2, HgCl2, Ni(PO3)2, Pb(NO3), and CuCl2. 2H2O). The selected metal ions are among the most harmful water pollutants.

For the evaluation of the CNDs selectivity toward the above ions, 200 μl of CNDs (D-CNDs) and (O-CNDs) were added to 1.3 ml of deionized water and the fluorescence was tested at λem = 450 nm (λex = 360 nm) and recorded as F0 (blank fluorescence). Analogously, 200 μl of CNDs solution was added to 1.3 ml of different metal ions solutions with a final concentration of 200 μM of metal. (CNDs concentration was kept constant). Samples were first vortexed and then left incubated for 5 min. The fluorescence was measured and recorded as F. For quantitative determination of D-CNDs and O-CNDs as Fe3+ sensors based on fluorescence quenching for each of the prepared CNDs, 200 μl of 1.7 mg/ml CNDs have been added to 1.3 ml of Fe3+ salt solution having different concentrations ranging from 0 to 400 μM. The fluorescence of the solution was measured as reported above. Different sensing parameters have been estimated such as sensing range, sensitivity, limit of detection (LOD, see Eq. (2)), and limit of quantification (LOQ Eq. (3)).

The performance of both CNDs probes (D-CNDs and O-CNDs) for Fe3+ detection has been studied in natural water sources (tap, rain, and drinking) by spiking 20 μM and 50 μM of Fe3+ into collected water containing 200 μl of CNDs. The performance was evaluated by calculating %Recovery, and, %RSD.
(2)
L O D =
3 σ
m
Where σ is the standard error of blank and m is the slope of the linear fit.
(3)
L O Q =
10 σ
m

Tobacco Molasses consists of tobacco and molasses that contains sugar, flavor, a trace amount of dye, and glycerin. Used Tobacco Molasses was used as a precursor for the synthesis of D-CNDs and O-CNDs. Herein, the utilization of smoked tobacco molasses as a precursor for the synthesis of CNDs can align with several green chemistry principles, especially those related to the utilization of renewable resources, waste reduction, minimizing hazardous material, and water-based synthesis [35].

It is thought that first molasses is consistently pyrolyzed during smoking into carbon-based materials, forming CNDs stacked on the burned tobacco. When the burned tobacco was soaked in water, the CNDs that had formed were extracted and dispersed in the water and considered as directly formed CNDs (D-CNDs) [36]. The CNDs, through this step, were produced with a product yield of 54.7 %. In the second step, the dried residual solid material obtained after the extraction was subjected to oxidation to form CNDs through a top-down method. The breaking down of carbon-based material to CNDs was assisted by hydrogen peroxide, which helps to form nanometric particles with surfaces rich in oxygenated groups. The CNDs, through this step, were produced with a product yield of 17 % and named O-CNDs.
AFM was used to study the morphology and the size of both CNDs, as shown in Fig. 2 a-c, D-CNDs show a broad particle size ranging from 2.9 to 33 nm with an average of 10.7 nm.
On the other hand, O-CNDs show a narrow particle size ranging from 0.93 to 5.9 with an average of 2.7 nm, as shown in Fig. 2 d-e.

FT-IR and XPS Spectroscopy were used to determine the chemical composition and surface functional groups of D-CNDs and O-CNDs. The FT-IR spectrum of D-CNDs illustrated in Fig. 3 a (black) shows peaks at 3342, 2927, 1734, 1578, 1410,1053, and 815 cm−1 which could be related to OH, CH, CO, CC, O–H bending, C–O–C, and C–H bending, respectively [8,37]. On the other hand, the FT-IR spectrum of O-CNDs in Fig. 3a (red) exhibits nearly similar peaks of D-CNDs, however, certain peaks show a slight shift with respect to wavenumber, and due to oxidation occurred by H2O2, there is a significant increase in the intensity of oxygen groups such as O–H, and C–O–C. The enhancement in oxygen groups of O-CNDs was also proved by the surface negative charge as PALS ζ-potential which has changed from −10.3 mV for D-CNDs to −15.2 mV for O-CNDs. The values of ζ-potential indicate that the synthesized CNDs are well-dispersed in water [8].

Moreover, the XPS acquisition was conducted for both CNDs. The XPS survey for D-CNDs and O-CNDs have been illustrated in Fig. 3b and c, respectively, showing that D-CNDs are composed of carbon, and oxygen with atomic percentages of 70.7, and 29.3 respectively, on the other hand, the O-CNDs are also formed of carbon, and oxygen with atomic percentages of 34.4, and 65.6, respectively (Fig. 3c), the significant increase in oxygen content (from 29.3 to 65.6 %) is due to oxidation occurred by the incorporation of hydrogen peroxide introduced during synthesis of O-CNDs. For D-CNDs and O-CNDs, the high-resolution C 1s core (Fig. 4 a &c) can be deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.8, 286.3, 287.5, and 288.9 eV which can be related to C–C, C–O, CO, and O–CO [38,39], respectively. The main difference that can be observed between both CNDs is a significant reduction in the C–C peak, it was 54.2 % for D-CNDs and 19.1 % for O-CNDs, this difference could be attributed to the oxidation action that occurred by the hydrogen peroxide used in the preparation of O-CNDs, this could also be approved by the deconvolution of high-resolution O 1s core as shown in Fig. 4b and d, two peaks have been resulted, O–C and OC [40] with a high concentration of C–O in O-CNDs (47.7 %) compared to D-CNDs (4.2 %) These results are well matched with FT-IR acquisition, indicating a clear difference in the concentration of oxygen groups on the surfaces of both CNDs which would play a role in enhancing the sensing parameters of metallic ions in water.

The optical properties of synthesized CNDs have been studied by UV–Vis and photoluminescence. Fig. 5 a shows the UV–Vis of D-CNDs with high absorption in the UV–Vis region and a tail extending into the visible one. A peak at 280 nm is shown and could be ascribed to n-π* attributed to CO groups [22].

On the other hand, in Fig. 5c, the UV–Vis spectrum of O-CNDs exhibits the same behavior with UV absorption as a shoulder and tail extending in the visible region. The indirect optical energy band gap could be calculated by converting the UV–Vis absorption spectrum into a plot
(
a h v
)
0.5
of versus
h v
, where
h
is Planck's constant,
a
is the absorption coefficient, and
h v
is equal to 1240/wavelength. The linear region of the Tauc plot is extrapolated to the x-axis, estimating the energy band gap in electron volts (eV). The calculated band gap of D-CNDs corresponds to a value of 2.25 eV as in Fig. 5b, while the O-CNDs give a higher band gap of 3 eV (see Fig. 5d). This increase in band gap can be attributed to the significant decrease in particle size and the nature of CNDs surface chemistry [22].

The synthesized CNDs fluorescence is excitation-dependent, as shown in Fig. 6 . This phenomenon is well-known for CNDs and can be attributed to surface defects, functional groups, different particle sizes, and different energy levels of emissive traps [41,42]. Both CNDs (D-CNDs and O-CNDs) show a maximum emission at 445 nm when excited at 340 nm. O-CNDs show an emission intensity 2.5-fold better than D-CNDs, which would be related to the surface defects created during oxidation and/or due to the existence of more oxygenated functional groups [43]. Moreover, the quantum yield has been calculated according to Eq. (1) . D-CNDs have a quantum yield of 1.55 %, while O-CNDs exhibit a higher Q. Y, which was 3.1 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing activity of the synthesized CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the unique properties of the synthesized CNDs, they could exhibit a significant role in environmental monitoring and resource recovery. CNDs can be good candidates as sensors for the detection of environmental pollutants such as ions, molecules, and biomolecules [44]. In addition, CNDs can serve as imaging tools for real-time visualization of pollutants and as photocatalysts for air and water purification addressing pollution challenges [45]. In resource recovery, functionalized CNDs facilitate the selective adsorption of metal ions, enabling the recovery of valuable metals from industrial effluents and electronic waste. Additionally, CNDs find application in wastewater treatment, energy storage, and carbon capture, showcasing their versatility in sustainable practices [46].

In sensing metal ions in a water environment, ions can interact with CNDs' surface functional groups, altering their fluorescence intensity. Therefore, we estimated the efficiency of the CNDs synthesized from Tobacco Molasses as sensors for cations detection. As found previously the synthesized CNDs; D-CNDs (from directly burned molasses) and O-CNDs (obtained after oxidation of residual carbonized tobacco) have different optical properties, sizes, and functional groups which would result in different sensing abilities and varied sensing parameters. The most important feature is the difference in the concentration of oxygen groups which could affect the binding affinity and interaction with the detected cation [47]. First, we looked at how the CNDs' fluorescence changed when various cations were present. These cations were Fe3+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, and Pb2+. The fluorescence emission intensity of the synthesized CNDs is remarkably high in the absence of metal ions. Variations in the fluorescence intensities have been seen after the cations were spiked [48].

As illustrated in (Fig. 7 ). Fe3+ and Fe2+ act as the most efficient quenchers for D-CNDs and O-CNDs. In fact, for D-CNDs, 200 μM of Fe3+ induces a quenching in the emission spectra equal to 56 % of the original intensity and 51.3 % of the original intensity of O-CNDs.

However, spiking with the same amount of other metal ions shows little to no quenching in the fluorescence emission spectrum except Fe2+, which shows a quenching of 28 % and 45 % for D-CNDs and O-CNDs, respectively.

Subsequently, the sensitivity of the O-CNDs and D-CNDs sensors to Fe3+ ions has been studied. In the presence of Fe3+ in the range of 0–400 μM concentrations, the fluorescence of CNDs has been monitored. A gradual decay in the fluorescence intensity is noticed upon increasing Fe3+ concentration as shown in Fig. 8 a and b.

The plot of quenching efficiency (F0–F/F0) versus Fe3+ concentration has been constructed for both CNDs as shown in Fig. 8c, which display good linearity in the range of 0–50 μM (Fig. 8d), where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of CNDs at 450 nm (the maximum emission obtained at excitation wavelength 360 nm) in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively.

Fig. 8d shows calibration curves for D-CNDs, and O-CNDs, these curves can be fitted with good linearity. Different sensing parameters can be estimated for both CNDs probes such as linear sensing range, sensitivity, LOD, and LOQ, these parameters are summarized in Table 1 .

As shown in Table 1, O-CNDs show better results concerning sensitivity, LOD, LOQ, and efficiency than D-CNDs. This behavior could be attributed to different reasons such as the particle size, oxygenated surface functional groups, and charge. O-CNDs have smaller sizes than D-CNDs and this will result in larger surface areas-to volume-ratio, leading to more available sites for ion interaction with formed CNDs and that would affect positively the ion sensing ability and improve the sensor parameters [49]. On the other hand, O-CNDs have more oxygen groups such as hydroxyl, and carboxyl which can act as binding sites for cations, influencing the sensitivity and selectivity of the sensor [50]. Moreover, these groups would modify the surface charge of CNDs as found for O-CNDs (more negative) compared to D-CNDs. Furthermore, the surface charge plays an important role in cation sensing by enhancing the electrostatic interaction between carbon dots and detected ions [51,52]. The above reasons would improve the binding affinity between Fe3+ and O-CNDs and show better sensing parameters than D-CNDs.

The performance of both CNDs (O-CNDs and D-CNDs) probes for Fe3+ detection has been evaluated as well in environmental water samples: tap water, rainwater, and drinking water. The natural samples were centrifuged and filtered before analysis to prevent the adverse effects of big solid particles.

The fluorescence intensity was recorded after the water samples were spiked with varying concentrations of Fe3+ solution (20 and 50 μM), and the Fe3+ concentration was calculated using the obtained linear formulas from the calibration curve. (Fig. 8d). Results represented in Table 2 show good recoveries (comprised between 92.66 and 104.85) and high analytical precision (with %RSD from 0.23 to 3.34, n = 3).

According to the World Health Organization (WHO), the maximum allowed water concentration is 5.36 μM [53]. The LOD for O-CNDs Probe is found to be lower than this limit. This foresees a possible application in determining Fe concentration in environmental water pools [54]. Moreover, the linear range of this probe can cover the content of Fe3+ in mammalian cells (total cell Fe3+ concentration = 20–100 μM) and human plasma (18 μM), allowing a potential application in the determination of iron in the biological systems [31].

Understanding the quenching mechanisms of synthesized CNDs that occur in the presence of Fe3+ ions is essential. Depending on the nanoparticles' functional groups and surface charges, the mechanism can vary according to the nature of the combination between interacted moieties [55]. The most common mechanism is static, related to the interaction between the quencher and the CNDs in the ground state, preventing fluorescent irradiation emission. The other mechanism is dynamic due to energy charge transfer between the quencher and the fluorescent nanoparticles. In some cases, a combined mechanism could be observed [56]. A Stern Volmer plot could be constructed to study which component is more dominant. For both CNDs, the plotted data are fitted by the modified Stern Volmer equation [57,58], Eq. (4) :
(4)
F 0
F
= exp
(
b
[ Q ]
a
Where [Q] is the concentration of the quenching ion (i.e. Fe3+], a and b are constants. Both plots for O-CNDs and D-CNDs are fitted according to the above equation with higher R-square values (Fig. 9 ), indicating a combined (static-dynamic) quenching mechanism where the static is dominant due to the values of a constant nearly equal to 1. The dynamic effect occurs at high concentration levels only with a shift shown nearly at 200 μM of Fe3+ concentration.

It is worthy to compare the synthesized CNDs (D-CNDs, and O-CNDs) as Fe3+ sensors with other CNDs synthesized from wastes or natural resources as summarized in Table 3 . As shown The CNDs are competitive sensors, besides the alignment with the principles of circular economy and green chemistry principles achieved by using waste to get useful products playing a role in environmental monitoring.

As reported previously, the synthesized CNDs from tobacco molasses precursor exhibit unique properties and competitive sensing parameters. However, there is a window for future research to improve these CNDs, the future prospectives could be related to modifying the synthesis methods, functionalizing or doping of synthesized CNDs, and/or hybridization with other nanomaterials such as Ag, Au, and ZnO. All these steps could improve the surface properties (morphology, chemical composition, and optical properties) of the CNDs enhancing their affinity with other ions, molecules, and biomolecules and enhance their selectivity and other sensing parameters.

In this study, CNDs were synthesized from smoked tobacco molasses through sequential and subsequent extraction and chemical oxidation processes. Two types of CNDs have been resulted (O-CNDs and D-CNDs) with diverse particle size, fluorescence, surface chemistry, and charge which make them good candidates for various applications. The utilization of tobacco molasses aligns with circular economy principles by repurposing athewaste for value-added products designed for specific applications, resulting in reducing waste, and enhancing resource efficiency and process effectiveness.

The sequential synthesis process of our CNDs reflects efficiency and resource optimization in line with green chemistry. Moreover, the resulting quenching responses of synthesized CNDs to iron ions demonstrate their potential in selective ion sensing which aligns also with green chemistry principles by offering a selective, sensitive, and efficient detection method without the use of complex and hazardous processes.

It can be concluded that both CNDs, D-CNDs, and O-CNDs have a detection response to iron ions with superior sensing performance of O-CNDs toward Fe3+ ion, this better performance of O-CNDs could be ascribed to smaller particle size, higher oxygen functional groups, and surface negative charge which would play an important role in enhancing the cation interaction and binding affinity with CNDs surface.

This study does not only contribute insights into CNDs synthesis, but also exemplifies the integration of circular economy and green chemistry principles in developing functional nanomaterials for various applications such as ion sensing.

Shadi Sawalha: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Mohyeddin Assali: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Amal Yaseen: Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Aya Ataya: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Leena Refai: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Rinad Hamed: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation. Giuseppe Misia: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources. Silvia Collavini: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources. Alessandro Silvestri: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yao, Q.F., et al., Directly reusing waste fish scales for facile, large-scale and green extraction of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their application in sensing of ferric ions. Sustainable Chemistry and Pharmacy, 2020. 17: p. 100305.https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scp.2020.100305

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A green approach for synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots from waste engine oil: a strategy for waste to value added products
Diam. Relat. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K, K., B.M. V, and N. P, A green approach for synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots from waste engine oil: A strategy for waste to value added products. Diamond and Related Materials, 2022. 121: p. 108724.https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2021.108724

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent nano-biomass dots: ultrasonic-assisted extraction and their application as nanoprobe for Fe3+ detection
Nanoscale Res. Lett.

Colloids Surf. A Physicochem. Eng. Asp.
